lessons.title: Introduction to Charcoal
lessons.instructions: Read the basics of what charcoal is and why grillers choose it. Click Next to continue.
learning_objectives: Understand how charcoal is produced and why it's used in grilling
Learn how charcoal creates the distinctive smoky flavor
Recognize that different charcoal types have different properties

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Charcoal is a carbon-rich material produced by slowly heating wood without oxygen. In grilling it delivers intense, even heat — far hotter than a gas flame can sustain over a full cook.
sections.media_alt: Charcoal burning with bright orange flames.

sections.heading: Flavor
sections.body: As charcoal burns it releases aromatic compounds that bond to the surface of whatever you are cooking. That is where the unmistakable smoky flavor of charcoal-grilled food comes from.
sections.media_alt: Steaks and vegetables grilling over glowing coals.

lessons.title: Types of Charcoal
lessons.instructions: Tap a charcoal type to open its detail page. Each type you finish is marked as studied when you return here.
learning_objectives: Identify four common types of charcoal used in grilling
Compare charcoal types by heat output, burn duration, and consistency
Choose the right charcoal type based on cooking needs

sections.heading: Four common options
sections.body: There is no single 'best' charcoal — the right choice depends on how long you need to cook and how hot you need to go.

types_of_charcoal.label: Briquettes
types_of_charcoal.summary: Uniform pillow-shaped lumps that burn predictably and are easy to light.
types_of_charcoal.description: Compressed sawdust and wood binders, pressed into uniform pillow shapes. Predictable, affordable, and easy to learn on.
types_of_charcoal.best_for: Weeknight grilling, burgers, steaks, and anyone who wants predictable, forgiving heat.
pros: Very predictable temperature across a cook
Affordable and available at any hardware store
Easy to light with a chimney starter
cons: Leaves more ash than natural charcoal
Won't reach the extreme high heat of lump

types_of_charcoal.label: Lump
types_of_charcoal.summary: Chunks of natural hardwood that light fast and burn very hot but run out quickly.
types_of_charcoal.description: Chunks of natural hardwood that light quickly and burn much hotter than briquettes. Irregular pieces make temperature control harder.
types_of_charcoal.best_for: Searing steaks, smash burgers, and any cook where you want maximum heat in a short window.
pros: Reaches very high temperatures
Lights quickly and produces little ash
100% natural — no binders or additives
cons: Burns out fast, not great for long cooks
Inconsistent piece sizes make temperature control harder

types_of_charcoal.label: Competition Briquettes
types_of_charcoal.summary: Premium briquettes that hold a long, steady, high heat for hours of low-and-slow cooking.
types_of_charcoal.description: Competition briquettes are the premium tier: denser, cleaner-burning, and engineered for the marathon cooks you see at BBQ competitions. They hold a high, steady temperature for hours with almost no babysitting.
types_of_charcoal.best_for: Ribs, brisket, pork shoulder — anything you want to cook low and slow for four hours or more.
pros: Longest burn duration of any common charcoal
Extremely consistent — set it and forget it
Clean burn, very little ash
cons: Overkill for a quick weeknight cook

types_of_charcoal.label: Bamboo
types_of_charcoal.summary: Eco-friendly bamboo charcoal with a distinct aroma and a moderately long burn.
types_of_charcoal.description: Bamboo charcoal is made from fast-growing bamboo, a more sustainable source than hardwood. It burns a little cooler than briquettes but produces a distinctive aroma that pairs beautifully with seafood and vegetables.
types_of_charcoal.best_for: Seafood, grilled vegetables, and cooks where you want a cleaner, subtly sweet smoke flavor.
pros: Renewable — bamboo regrows in a few years
Distinct, pleasant aroma
Moderate burn time fits most backyard cooks
cons: Harder to find in standard grocery stores
Doesn't get as hot as lump for searing

lessons.title: Grilling Techniques
lessons.instructions: Tap a technique to learn how to set it up and what to cook on it.
learning_objectives: Master four fundamental charcoal grilling layouts
Understand the heat characteristics and ideal uses of each technique
Match cooking styles to the best layout for your next cook

sections.heading: Four layouts for every cook
sections.body: Each layout delivers heat differently. Go all-in on one, or mix and match depending on what you're cooking.

techniques.label: Two-Zone
techniques.summary: Coals on one side and an empty grate on the other for the most forgiving all-rounder.
techniques.description: Coals on one side, empty grate on the other. You get a hot zone for searing and a cool zone for gentle finishing.
techniques.best_for: Chicken thighs, bone-in cuts, thick steaks, burgers, vegetables — anything where you want a good sear without burning it.
pros: Most forgiving layout for beginners
Works for fast cooks and medium cooks alike
Easy to adjust temperature on the fly by moving food
cons: Takes more babysitting than Snake Method
Not ideal for truly low-and-slow cooking

techniques.label: Snake Method
techniques.summary: Coals in a C-shape lit at one end that burns slowly for hours of steady, low heat.
techniques.description: Arrange unlit coals in a C-shape, light one end, and close the lid. The burn creeps slowly around the snake for hours of low, steady heat.
techniques.best_for: Ribs, brisket, pork shoulder, whole chicken — any low-and-slow cook that runs 4 hours or longer.
pros: Produces hours of steady, low heat
Minimal temperature fluctuation
No need to add charcoal or adjust vents constantly
cons: Takes precision to get the initial temperature right

techniques.label: Direct Heat
techniques.summary: Coals spread evenly under the entire grate for maximum heat everywhere.
techniques.description: Spread coals evenly under the entire grate for maximum heat everywhere. Perfect for fast cooks that benefit from a hard char.
techniques.best_for: Burgers, hot dogs, sausages, shrimp, grilled vegetables — fast cooks where speed and color matter more than gentle heat.
pros: Maximum heat everywhere
Fastest setup of any layout
Creates a beautiful, even char
cons: Can easily dry out thick cuts if not careful
No cool zone to move food to if it's burning

techniques.label: Charcoal + Wood
techniques.summary: Lit charcoal base topped with hardwood chunks to add deep smoke flavor.
techniques.description: Add hardwood chunks (oak, hickory, cherry) on top of lit coals to release smoke. Quick smoke flavor without the 12-hour cook time.
techniques.best_for: Smoked chicken wings, pork shoulder, brisket — any cut where flavor matters and you want the speed of charcoal with the taste of smoke.
pros: Adds deep smoke flavor quickly
Works on any charcoal grill
Faster than traditional offset smokers
cons: Requires soaking wood chunks first (optional but recommended)
Not as smoky as dedicated smokers

sections.heading: 1. Cool the coals
sections.body: Close vents and let coals cool. They stay hot 24 hours—don't assume they're out.
sections.media_alt: Glowing charcoal coals cooling down in a grill.

sections.heading: 2. Extinguish with water
sections.body: Pour water on coals and stir until no steam or embers remain.
sections.media_alt: Pouring water on hot charcoal coals to extinguish them.

sections.heading: 3. Dispose safely
sections.body: Cool ashes go in a metal container, then regular trash.
sections.media_alt: Metal container with cooled charcoal ashes ready for disposal.

quiz.question: Which charcoal lasts the longest?
explanations: Briquettes burn for 3–4 hours, which is long, but not the longest.
Lump charcoal burns out quickly in 1–1.5 hours.
Correct! Competition Briquettes are premium charcoal designed for long, steady burns of 4–6 hours.
Bamboo burns for 2–3 hours, moderate duration.
quiz.key_takeaway: Competition Briquettes are engineered for marathon cooks.

quiz.question: Which charcoal burns the hottest?
explanations: Briquettes peak at 650–700°F.
Correct! Lump charcoal reaches 800–1000°F, the hottest of all types.
Competition Briquettes peak at 700–800°F, high but not the hottest.
Bamboo reaches 600–700°F.
quiz.key_takeaway: Lump charcoal is ideal for high-heat searing.

quiz.question: Which charcoal is the most consistent?
explanations: Briquettes are 95% consistent, very reliable.
Lump is only 60–75% consistent because it's chunks of natural wood.
Correct! Competition Briquettes are 95–98% consistent, the most reliable for precise temperature control.
Bamboo is 80–90% consistent.
quiz.key_takeaway: Competition Briquettes offer the best predictability.

quiz.question: You are cooking ribs for several hours and need steady, low heat. Which charcoal layout should you use?
explanations: Direct Heat spreads coals everywhere for maximum heat—too hot for a long, slow cook.
Two-Zone is great for searing and finishing, but doesn't provide the steady, low heat ribs need.
Correct! The Snake Method creates a slow burn that creeps around the grill, perfect for hours of low, steady heat.
Charcoal + Wood Mix adds flavor but isn't the best for sustained low heat over many hours.
quiz.key_takeaway: The Snake Method is the go-to for low-and-slow cooking like ribs and brisket.

quiz.question: In the U.S., which waste category should used charcoal be disposed in?
quiz.hero_caption: Used charcoal — once fully cooled — heads straight to the trash, not recycling or compost.
explanations: Charcoal is not recyclable.
Charcoal is not compostable.
Correct! Used charcoal goes in the general trash once it is completely cool.
Charcoal only goes in the trash—not the other categories.
quiz.key_takeaway: Cool, used charcoal is regular waste, not a special material.
